27	func (f *CommandFactory) newAgentExecuteBeadCommand() *cobra.Command {
28		cmd := &cobra.Command{
29			Use:   "execute-bead <bead-id>",
30			Short: "Run an agent on one bead in an isolated worktree, then land the result",
31			Long: `execute-bead is the primitive: it runs a single agent on a single bead in
32	an isolated git worktree (the worker step), then hands the result to the
33	parent-side orchestrator which merges or preserves the commits.
34	
35	The worker classifies its outcome as one of:
36	  task_succeeded  — agent exited 0 and produced commits
37	  task_no_changes — agent exited 0 but made no commits
38	  task_failed     — agent exited non-zero
39	
40	The orchestrator then applies landing logic (merge, gate evaluation, preserve)
41	and the final result reflects both the worker and landing decisions.
42	
43	For normal queue-driven work use "ddx work", which claims ready beads,
44	calls this command, and closes or unclaims each bead based on the result.
45	Reach for execute-bead directly only to debug or re-run a specific bead.
46	
47	Result status is reported on the "status:" line and is one of:
48	  success                          — merged (or preserved with --no-merge)
49	  no_changes                       — agent ran but produced no diff
50	  no_evidence_produced             — agent exited with no commit and no rationale
51	  already_satisfied                — closed by the loop after repeated no_changes
52	  land_conflict                    — merge failed; result preserved
53	  post_run_check_failed            — checks failed; result preserved
54	  execution_failed                 — agent or harness error
55	  structural_validation_failed     — bead or prompt inputs invalid
56	
57	ddx work closes the bead with session/commit evidence only on success
58	(and already_satisfied); every other status leaves the bead open and
59	unclaimed for a later attempt.`,
60			Example: `  # Debug one specific bead (prefer "ddx work" for normal queue work)
61	  ddx agent execute-bead ddx-7a01ba6c
62	
63	  # Run against a non-HEAD base revision
64	  ddx agent execute-bead ddx-7a01ba6c --from main
65	
66	  # Preserve the result under refs/ddx/iterations/... instead of merging
67	  ddx agent execute-bead ddx-7a01ba6c --no-merge
68	
69	  # Pin harness/model for a debugging pass

91	func (f *CommandFactory) runAgentExecuteBead(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
92		beadID := args[0]
93		if !validBeadID.MatchString(beadID) {
94			return fmt.Errorf("invalid bead ID %q: must contain only letters, digits, dots, underscores, and hyphens", beadID)
95		}
96		projectFlag, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("project")
97		fromRev, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("from")
98		noMerge, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("no-merge")
99		harness, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("harness")
100		model, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("model")
101		provider, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("provider")
102		modelRef, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("model-ref")
103		effort, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("effort")
104		contextBudget, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("context-budget")
105		promptFile, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("prompt")
106		requestTimeout, _ := cmd.Flags().GetDuration("request-timeout")
107		minPower, _ := cmd.Flags().GetInt("min-power")
108		maxPower, _ := cmd.Flags().GetInt("max-power")
109		asJSON, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("json")
110	
111		projectRoot := resolveProjectRoot(projectFlag, f.WorkingDir)
112	
113		overrides := config.CLIOverrides{
114			Harness:       harness,
115			Model:         model,
116			Provider:      provider,
117			ModelRef:      modelRef,
118			Effort:        effort,
119			ContextBudget: contextBudget,
120			MinPower:      minPower,
121			MaxPower:      maxPower,
122		}
123		if requestTimeout > 0 {
124			overrides.ProviderRequestTimeout = &requestTimeout
125		}
126		rcfg, err := config.LoadAndResolve(projectRoot, overrides)
127		if err != nil {
128			return fmt.Errorf("load config at %s: %w", projectRoot, err)
129		}
130		runtime := agent.ExecuteBeadRuntime{
131			FromRev:    fromRev,
132			PromptFile: promptFile,
133			WorkerID:   os.Getenv("DDX_WORKER_ID"),
134			BeadEvents: bead.NewStore(filepath.Join(projectRoot, ".ddx")),
135		}
136	
137		var gitOps agent.GitOps = &agent.RealGitOps{}
138		if f.executeBeadGitOverride != nil {
139			gitOps = f.executeBeadGitOverride
140		}
141	
142		var orchestratorGitOps agent.OrchestratorGitOps = &agent.RealOrchestratorGitOps{}
143		if f.executeBeadOrchestratorGitOverride != nil {
144			orchestratorGitOps = f.executeBeadOrchestratorGitOverride
145		}
146	
147		// Test injection seam: AgentRunnerOverride feeds canned *Result values
148		// from fakes without spinning up a real provider. Production callers
149		// leave this nil so ExecuteBead constructs an agent service from
150		// projectRoot internally.
151		if f.AgentRunnerOverride != nil {
152			runtime.AgentRunner = f.AgentRunnerOverride
153		}
154	
155		// Preflight the orphan-model check before creating a worktree. Mirrors
156		// the queue-work preflight so `ddx agent execute-bead --model <unroutable>`
157		// errors before any git work happens rather than mid-execution. Skip the
158		// preflight when a test runner override is in use — overrides may be
159		// fakes that bypass routing entirely.
160		if f.AgentRunnerOverride == nil {
161			if err := agent.ValidateForExecuteLoopViaService(cmd.Context(), f.WorkingDir, harness, model, provider, modelRef); err != nil {
162				return err
163			}
164		}
165	
166		// Recover any orphaned worktrees from previous crashed runs before
167		// spawning a new worker. This is the parent's responsibility.
168		agent.RecoverOrphans(gitOps, projectRoot, beadID)
169	
170		// Worker step: run the agent in an isolated worktree.
171		res, err := agent.ExecuteBeadWithConfig(cmd.Context(), projectRoot, beadID, rcfg, runtime, gitOps)
172		if err != nil && res == nil {
173			return err
174		}
175	
176		// Orchestrator step: land the result (ff or merge → push, gate eval,
177		// preserve). Always run when we have a result — the orchestrator handles
178		// all cases including no-changes and error (agent failed with no commits).
179		//
180		// Single-bead CLI is strictly serial (no sibling workers in this
181		// process), so we can wire the LandingAdvancer to call Land() directly
182		// without going through a per-project coordinator goroutine. Tests may
183		// inject a custom advancer via f.executeBeadLandingAdvancerOverride.
184		if res != nil {
185			var advancer func(r *agent.ExecuteBeadResult) (*agent.LandResult, error)
186			if f.executeBeadLandingAdvancerOverride != nil {
187				advancer = f.executeBeadLandingAdvancerOverride
188			} else {
189				landingGitOps := landingGitOpsFromFactory(f)
190				advancer = func(r *agent.ExecuteBeadResult) (*agent.LandResult, error) {
191					return agent.Land(projectRoot, agent.BuildLandRequestFromResult(projectRoot, r), landingGitOps)
192				}
193			}
194			landingOpts := agent.BeadLandingOptions{
195				NoMerge:         noMerge,
196				LandingAdvancer: advancer,
197			}
198	
199			// Wire required execution gates: when the worker manifest declares
200			// governing IDs, gate-eval them in an ephemeral worktree at ResultRev
201			// before LandBeadResult decides merge vs preserve. The original
202			// worker worktree was cleaned up by ExecuteBead, so we pin ResultRev
203			// to a transient ref and check it out into a temp worktree for the
204			// duration of gate evaluation.
205			if res != nil && res.ResultRev != "" && res.ResultRev != res.BaseRev && res.ExitCode == 0 {
206				wt, ids, cleanup, ctxErr := agent.BuildLandingGateContext(projectRoot, res, gitOps)
207				if ctxErr != nil {
208					// Soft-fail: log and skip gate eval rather than abort the land.
209					fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "ddx: warning: gate-context setup failed: %v (skipping required-gate eval)\n", ctxErr)
210				} else if wt != "" {
211					defer cleanup()
212					landingOpts.WtPath = wt
213					landingOpts.GovernIDs = ids
214					landingOpts.ChecksArtifactPath = filepath.Join(projectRoot, res.ExecutionDir, "checks.json")
215					landingOpts.ChecksArtifactRel = filepath.Join(res.ExecutionDir, "checks.json")
216				}
217			}
218	
219			if landing, landErr := agent.LandBeadResult(projectRoot, res, orchestratorGitOps, landingOpts); landErr == nil {
220				agent.ApplyLandingToResult(res, landing)
221				_ = agent.WriteExecuteBeadResultArtifact(projectRoot, res)
222			} else if err == nil {
223				err = landErr
224			}
225		}
226	
227		if err != nil {
228			_ = writeExecuteBeadResult(cmd, res, asJSON)
229			return err
230		}
231	
232		return writeExecuteBeadResult(cmd, res, asJSON)
233	}

235	func writeExecuteBeadResult(cmd *cobra.Command, res *agent.ExecuteBeadResult, asJSON bool) error {
236		if res == nil {
237			return nil
238		}
239		if asJSON {
240			enc := json.NewEncoder(cmd.OutOrStdout())
241			enc.SetIndent("", "  ")
242			return enc.Encode(res)
243		}
244	
245		fmt.Fprintf(cmd.OutOrStdout(), "bead:    %s\n", res.BeadID)
246		fmt.Fprintf(cmd.OutOrStdout(), "base:    %s\n", res.BaseRev)
247		if res.ResultRev != "" && res.ResultRev != res.BaseRev {
248			fmt.Fprintf(cmd.OutOrStdout(), "result:  %s\n", res.ResultRev)
249		}
250		fmt.Fprintf(cmd.OutOrStdout(), "outcome: %s\n", res.Outcome)
251		fmt.Fprintf(cmd.OutOrStdout(), "status:  %s\n", res.Status)
252		if res.Detail != "" {
253			fmt.Fprintf(cmd.OutOrStdout(), "detail:  %s\n", res.Detail)
254		}
255		if res.NoChangesRationale != "" {
256			fmt.Fprintf(cmd.OutOrStdout(), "rationale: %s\n", res.NoChangesRationale)
257		}
258		if res.PreserveRef != "" {
259			fmt.Fprintf(cmd.OutOrStdout(), "ref:     %s\n", res.PreserveRef)
260		}
261		return nil
262	}

10	// InfrastructureFailurePatterns are detail substrings that indicate a
11	// transient infrastructure-level failure (provider 502, network unreachable,
12	// command not found, auth/quota exhausted) rather than a model-capability
13	// failure (test failed, build failed, no changes after attempt).
14	//
15	// Infrastructure failures should not consume escalation budget — the bead
16	// should be deferred with a retry-after, not retried at the next tier with
17	// a more expensive model. The model wasn't given a fair chance.
18	//
19	// Patterns are matched case-insensitively as substrings of the failure
20	// detail string. Any new pattern added here is automatically picked up by
21	// IsInfrastructureFailure callers.
22	var InfrastructureFailurePatterns = []string{
23		// HTTP-level provider unavailability
24		"502", "503", "504",
25		"bad gateway",
26		"service unavailable",
27		"gateway timeout",
28		// Network-level unreachability
29		"connection refused",
30		"no such host",
31		"no route to host",
32		"network is unreachable",
33		"i/o timeout",
34		"context deadline exceeded",
35		// Auth/quota exhaustion (operator-fixable, not a model fault)
36		"401", "429",
37		"unauthorized",
38		"rate limit",
39		"ratelimit",
40		"quota exceeded",
41		"insufficient quota",
42		"insufficient_quota",
43		// Subprocess harness binary missing or unstartable
44		"command not found",
45		"executable file not found",
46		"no such file or directory",
47		// Upstream routing/service selection failures
48		"resolveroute:",
49		"no viable routing candidate",
50		"no live provider supports",
51		"no candidate satisfying local endpoint",
52	}
53	
54	// IsInfrastructureFailure reports whether the given failure status + detail
55	// indicates a transient infrastructure problem the model could not have
56	// fixed. Only execution_failed can be infrastructure; other escalatable
57	// statuses are semantic outcomes that should proceed through the tier ladder.
58	// Returns false for statuses whose detail does not match any known
59	// infrastructure pattern.
60	//
61	// Used by the execute-loop to decide whether to (a) defer the bead with a
62	// retry-after and try the same tier later, or (b) burn through to the next
63	// tier as the standard escalation policy.
64	func IsInfrastructureFailure(status, detail string) bool {
65		if status != "execution_failed" {
66			return false
67		}
68		if detail == "" {
69			return false
70		}
71		lower := strings.ToLower(detail)
72		for _, p := range InfrastructureFailurePatterns {
73			if strings.Contains(lower, p) {
74				return true
75			}
76		}
77		return false
78	}
79	
80	// DefaultMaxCostUSD is the per-execute-loop dollar cap on accumulated billed
81	// spend. Exceeding the cap halts further bead claiming so an execute-loop
82	// run cannot silently burn through arbitrary openrouter / pay-per-token
83	// credits. Local LLMs and subscription-bundled providers do not count
84	// toward the cap (see CountsTowardCostCap).
85	//
86	// $100 is large enough not to interrupt normal subscription-driven work
87	// (where billed cost stays at $0) and small enough to surface a clear stop
88	// signal before runaway openrouter usage.
89	const DefaultMaxCostUSD = 100.0
90	
91	// DefaultMaxBeadCostUSD is the default per-bead budget. When a single bead
92	// exhausts this amount across all escalation tiers, escalation stops and the
93	// bead is returned to open without a cooldown. Operators can override
94	// per-bead with a budget:<USD> label (e.g. budget:25.0).
95	//
96	// $5 is a conservative default — enough to attempt two or three smart-tier
97	// runs before halting, but low enough to protect against runaway escalation
98	// on a pathologically difficult bead.
99	const DefaultMaxBeadCostUSD = 5.0
100

170	// true (count) so accidentally-missing harness metadata never silently

173		if harnessName == "" {

175		}
176		t.mu.Lock()
177		if v, ok := t.cache[harnessName]; ok {
178			t.mu.Unlock()
179			return v
180		}
181		t.mu.Unlock()
182		result := true
183		if t.Lookup != nil {
184			result = t.Lookup(harnessName)

263		}

265		if spent < t.inner.MaxUSD {
266			return "", false
267		}
268		return fmt.Sprintf("%s: $%.2f billed >= $%.2f per-bead budget; set --max-bead-cost=0 to disable or add a budget:<USD> label to override", PerBeadBudgetExhaustedReason, spent, t.inner.MaxUSD), true

271	// ParseBeadBudgetLabel scans a bead's label slice for a "budget:<float>" entry
272	// and returns the parsed value and true when found. An entry with an invalid
273	// numeric suffix is ignored (returns 0, false) so the caller falls back to the
274	// default per-bead budget rather than disabling the budget entirely.
275	func ParseBeadBudgetLabel(labels []string) (float64, bool) {
276		const prefix = "budget:"
277		for _, l := range labels {
278			if !strings.HasPrefix(l, prefix) {
279				continue
280			}
281			raw := strings.TrimPrefix(l, prefix)
282			v, err := strconv.ParseFloat(raw, 64)
283			if err != nil || v < 0 {
284				return 0, false
285			}
286			return v, true
287		}
288		return 0, false
289	}

76	// ResolveTriagePolicy returns the effective triage policy for this config,

78	// ladders. Unknown failure-mode names and unknown action names are
79	// silently dropped (schema validation surfaces typos at load time).

81		policy := triage.DefaultPolicy()
82		if c == nil || c.Triage == nil || len(c.Triage.Policies) == 0 {
83			return policy
84		}
85		for modeStr, actionStrs := range c.Triage.Policies {
86			mode, err := triage.ParseFailureMode(modeStr)
87			if err != nil {

97		}

103		}

18	type ladderResponse struct {
19		next int
20		err  error
21	}

23	type ladderSpy struct {
24		mu        sync.Mutex
25		calls     []int
26		responses []ladderResponse
27	}
28	
29	func (s *ladderSpy) Next(actualPower int) (int, error) {
30		s.mu.Lock()
31		defer s.mu.Unlock()
32		s.calls = append(s.calls, actualPower)
33		idx := len(s.calls) - 1
34		if idx >= len(s.responses) {
35			return 0, tierescalation.ErrLadderExhausted
36		}
37		resp := s.responses[idx]
38		return resp.next, resp.err
39	}
40	
41	func (s *ladderSpy) CalledWith() []int {
42		s.mu.Lock()
43		defer s.mu.Unlock()
44		out := make([]int, len(s.calls))
45		copy(out, s.calls)
46		return out
47	}

226	// accumulated per-bead billed cost reaches the configured budget, escalation
227	// stops and the returned report signals per-bead budget exhaustion.
228	func TestPerBeadCostTracker_StopsEscalationAtLimit(t *testing.T) {
229		ladder := &ladderSpy{responses: []ladderResponse{
230			{next: 70},
231			{next: 90},
232		}}
233		attempts := 0
234	
235		// Budget is $3. First attempt costs $2 (below), second costs $2 (total $4 ≥ $3).
236		tracker := policyescalation.NewPerBeadCostTracker(3.0, nil)
237	
238		report, err := runEscalatingSingleTierAttempts(
239			context.Background(),
240			0,
241			ladder,
242			func(_ context.Context, requestedMinPower int) (agent.ExecuteBeadReport, error) {
243				attempts++
244				switch attempts {
245				case 1:
246					return agent.ExecuteBeadReport{
247						BeadID:      "ddx-per-bead-001",
248						ActualPower: 50,
249						Harness:     "openrouter",
250						CostUSD:     2.0,
251						Status:      agent.ExecuteBeadStatusExecutionFailed,
252						Detail:      "TestFoo failed",
253					}, nil
254				case 2:
255					return agent.ExecuteBeadReport{
256						BeadID:      "ddx-per-bead-001",
257						ActualPower: 70,
258						Harness:     "openrouter",
259						CostUSD:     2.0,
260						Status:      agent.ExecuteBeadStatusExecutionFailed,
261						Detail:      "TestFoo failed again",
262					}, nil
263				default:
264					t.Fatalf("unexpected attempt %d", attempts)
265					return agent.ExecuteBeadReport{}, nil
266				}
267			},
268			nil,
269			tracker,
270		)
271	
272		require.NoError(t, err)
273		assert.Equal(t, 2, attempts, "should stop after two attempts (second trips the budget)")
274		assert.Equal(t, agent.ExecuteBeadStatusExecutionFailed, report.Status)
275		assert.True(t, strings.Contains(report.Detail, policyescalation.PerBeadBudgetExhaustedReason),
276			"detail must contain per-bead budget exhausted marker, got: %q", report.Detail)
277		assert.Equal(t, 4.0, report.CostUSD, "CostUSD on the report should be the total bead spend")
278	}
279	
280	// TestPerBeadCostTracker_NoLimit_RunsToLadderExhaustion asserts that a
281	// nil PerBeadCostTracker (unlimited) does not interrupt escalation — the loop
282	// runs all the way through ladder exhaustion.
283	func TestPerBeadCostTracker_NoLimit_RunsToLadderExhaustion(t *testing.T) {
284		ladder := &ladderSpy{responses: []ladderResponse{
285			{next: 70},
286			// No more responses: second NextFloor call returns ErrLadderExhausted.
287		}}
288		attempts := 0
289	
290		report, err := runEscalatingSingleTierAttempts(
291			context.Background(),
292			0,
293			ladder,
294			func(_ context.Context, requestedMinPower int) (agent.ExecuteBeadReport, error) {
295				attempts++
296				return agent.ExecuteBeadReport{
297					BeadID:      "ddx-per-bead-002",
298					ActualPower: requestedMinPower,
299					Harness:     "openrouter",
300					CostUSD:     50.0, // high cost per attempt — should not stop with nil tracker
301					Status:      agent.ExecuteBeadStatusExecutionFailed,
302					Detail:      "TestFoo failed",
303				}, nil
304			},
305			nil,
306			nil, // no per-bead tracker = unlimited
307		)
308	
309		require.NoError(t, err)
310		assert.Equal(t, 2, attempts, "should run through all available rungs (ladder exhaustion, not budget)")
311		assert.False(t, strings.Contains(report.Detail, policyescalation.PerBeadBudgetExhaustedReason),
312			"detail must not contain per-bead budget exhausted marker when tracker is nil")
313	}

2	ddx:
3	  id: TD-037
4	  depends_on:
5	    - FEAT-006

11	# Technical Design: Bead-Level Execution Hints
12	
13	## Purpose
14	
15	DDx needs a durable way to say that a bead needs a stronger agent than the
16	default queue policy would infer. The mechanism must be portable across
17	machines and providers, auditable after the fact, and hard to cargo-cult into
18	concrete harness or model pins.
19	
20	This design defines bead-level execution hints as abstract power intent. It
21	does not let beads choose providers, harnesses, or concrete models.
22	
23	## Current State
24	
25	`ddx try` already has a partial mechanism:
26	
27	- Beads preserve unknown custom fields in `Extra`, but `ddx try` does not read
28	  arbitrary routing custom fields.
29	- `ddx try` accepts CLI execution constraints: abstract power bounds plus raw
30	  passthrough strings for profile, harness, provider, model, and model-ref.
31	- When no routing flags are supplied and no project routing config exists,
32	  `ddx try` calls `escalation.InferTier(bead)`.
33	- `InferTier` treats labels `tier:smart`, `tier:standard`, and `tier:cheap` as
34	  explicit tier overrides before falling back to priority, kind, and scope
35	  heuristics.
36	
37	That gives DDx a usable short-term path, but it is underspecified. It does not
38	define when `tier:smart` is justified, how the choice is audited, or how to
39	reject durable model/harness cargo culting.
40	
41	## Design
42	
43	### Durable Hint Surface
44	
45	DDx recognizes exactly one durable bead-level hint surface in v1:
46	
47	| Surface | Values | Meaning |
48	|---|---|---|
49	| `tier:cheap` label | cheap | Mechanical work where low-cost models should be enough. |
50	| `tier:standard` label | standard | Ordinary implementation or review work. |
51	| `tier:smart` label | smart | High-judgment, broad, ambiguous, or architecture-sensitive work. |
52	
53	The label is a request for abstract execution power. It is not a model name.
54	`tier:smart` maps to the current smart execution profile only at the DDx
55	request-construction boundary; Fizeau still owns the concrete route.
56	
57	DDx should not add bead-level `harness`, `provider`, `model`, or `model-ref`
58	fields. Those remain operator-supplied CLI passthrough constraints for one
59	attempt, or project/Fizeau configuration when a workspace intentionally pins
60	routing policy.
61	
62	### Smart-Tier Justification
63	
64	`tier:smart` requires a justification in the bead description. The justification
65	must name the reason the bead is likely capability-sensitive, using one or more
66	of these categories:
67	
68	- cross-cutting behavior or multiple subsystems;
69	- architecture, API, or data-model decision;
70	- ambiguous or competing requirements that need reconciliation;
71	- high-risk review, security, data-loss, or migration concern;
72	- evaluation of non-deterministic output quality;
73	- decomposition or planning work where weak reasoning creates downstream waste.
74	
75	Valid example:
76	
77	```text
78	SMART JUSTIFICATION: This bead decides the durable execution-hint contract and
79	its precedence against ADR-024 routing policy. A weak implementation could
80	persist concrete model pins into queue metadata.
81	```
82	
83	Invalid example:
84	
85	```text
86	SMART JUSTIFICATION: Claude worked well last time.
87	```
88	
89	The first enforcement step should live in the bead lifecycle lint hook so
90	authors and agents get feedback before dispatch. A later hardening pass may add
91	the same validation to `ddx bead create` and `ddx bead update`, but lint is the
92	minimum gate because all automated execution passes through it.
93	
94	### Precedence
95	
96	Execution intent resolves in this order:
97	
98	1. Explicit CLI flags for the current invocation.
99	2. Project/Fizeau routing configuration.
100	3. Durable bead hint label.
101	4. DDx heuristic inference from priority, kind, issue type, and scope.
102	5. DDx default.
103	
104	CLI flags win because they are operator actions for one attempt and are visible
105	in the command/evidence. Durable bead hints win over heuristics because they are
106	part of the reviewed work item.
107	
108	If a project has explicit routing configuration, `ddx try` must not silently
109	override it with a bead label. The label should still be recorded as requested
110	intent so operators can audit divergence between requested and actual routing.
111	
112	### Rejected Durable Route Pins
113	
114	Bead metadata must not persist concrete route choices. The lint hook should
115	reject or block execution when a bead includes durable fields or labels that
116	look like route pins, including:
117	
118	- `harness`, `agent-harness`, `execution-harness`, `try-harness`;
119	- `provider`, `agent-provider`, `execution-provider`, `try-provider`;
120	- `model`, `agent-model`, `execution-model`, `try-model`;
121	- `model-ref`, `agent-model-ref`, `execution-model-ref`, `try-model-ref`;
122	- labels such as `harness:claude`, `provider:openai`, or `model:gpt-...`.
123	
124	The diagnostic should say to use a one-off CLI flag for a reproduction or
125	explicit operator constraint:
126	
127	```bash
128	ddx try <id> --harness claude
129	```
130	
131	This keeps durable queue metadata portable and prevents agents from copying
132	route choices that happened to work once.
133	
134	### Audit Evidence
135	
136	Every `ddx try` / `ddx work` attempt should record routing-intent evidence
137	before execution starts. The evidence should be append-only and attached to the
138	attempt/run record, not only printed to stdout.
139	
140	Minimum fields:
141	
142	| Field | Meaning |
143	|---|---|
144	| `bead_id` | Target bead. |
145	| `attempt_id` | Attempt/run id. |
146	| `routing_intent_source` | `cli`, `project_config`, `bead_hint`, `heuristic`, or `default`. |
147	| `requested_tier` | `cheap`, `standard`, `smart`, or empty when not tier-based. |
148	| `requested_min_power` | Resolved `MinPower`, when available. |
149	| `requested_max_power` | Resolved `MaxPower`, when available. |
150	| `smart_justification` | Extracted justification text when `requested_tier=smart`. |
151	| `actual_harness` | Harness reported by Fizeau/agent after execution. |
152	| `actual_provider` | Provider reported after execution. |
153	| `actual_model` | Model reported after execution. |
154	| `actual_power` | Actual power reported after execution. |
155	| `routing_intent_degraded` | True when requested intent could not be met or was overridden. |
156	| `routing_intent_note` | Short reason for degradation or override. |
157	
158	The evidence event name should be stable, for example
159	`execution-routing-intent`. Existing attempt result fields already capture some
160	actual route facts; this event ties those facts back to why DDx requested that
161	power.
162	
163	### Metrics
164	
165	The agent metrics rollup should ingest the routing-intent fields once they are
166	present in run evidence. At minimum, operators need to answer:
167	
168	- How many attempts used `tier:smart`?
169	- Which beads requested smart, and what was the justification?
170	- What was the success rate and cost of smart-hinted work compared with
171	  heuristic or default work?
172	- How often did CLI or project config override a bead hint?
173	- Which beads carried rejected durable route pins?
174	- Which authors or agents are adding smart hints repeatedly?
175	
176	Initial metrics can be implemented by extending the normalized attempt
177	projection used by TD-032. A materialized rollup is not required.
178	
179	Suggested dimensions:
180	
181	| Dimension | Example |
182	|---|---|
183	| `routing_intent_source` | `bead_hint` |
184	| `requested_tier` | `smart` |
185	| `actual_power_bucket` | `>=80` |
186	| `degraded` | `true` |
187	| `bead_author` | `erik` or agent id when available |
188	| `smart_reason_category` | `architecture` |
189	
190	Suggested counters:
191	
192	- attempts by requested tier and source;
193	- success rate by requested tier;
194	- cost and token usage by requested tier;
195	- smart-hint count by bead author/agent;
196	- rejected durable route-pin count;
197	- override/degradation count.
198	
199	### Operator Reporting
200	
201	`ddx try` output should stay concise, but it should include the source when a
202	bead hint affects execution:
203	
204	```text
205	routing intent: tier=smart source=bead_hint
206	```
207	
208	If `tier:smart` is present without justification, the lint failure should be
209	plain:
210	
211	```text
212	bead uses tier:smart but has no SMART JUSTIFICATION section
213	```
214	
215	If a durable concrete pin is found:
216	
217	```text
218	bead metadata contains execution-model=gpt-5.5; durable model pins are not
219	allowed. Use ddx try <id> --model ... for one-off debugging.
220	```
221	
222	## Implementation Plan
223	
224	### Bead 1: specify and test hint parsing
225	
226	Scope:
227	
228	- Parse `tier:*` labels into a typed execution hint.
229	- Extract `SMART JUSTIFICATION:` from bead descriptions.
230	- Detect forbidden durable route-pin fields and labels.
231	
232	Acceptance:
233	
234	1. Tests cover valid `tier:cheap`, `tier:standard`, and `tier:smart`.
235	2. Tests cover missing smart justification.
236	3. Tests cover forbidden concrete route-pin fields and labels.
237	4. Existing heuristic inference behavior remains unchanged when no hint exists.
238	
239	### Bead 2: enforce hints in bead lifecycle lint
240	
241	Scope:
242	
243	- Add lint findings for missing smart justification.
244	- Add lint failures for durable concrete route pins.
245	- Keep diagnostics actionable.
246	
247	Acceptance:
248	
249	1. A smart bead without justification fails lint.
250	2. A bead with `harness:claude` or `execution-model=...` fails lint.
251	3. A bead with valid `tier:smart` and justification passes lint.
252	
253	### Bead 3: record routing-intent evidence
254	
255	Scope:
256	
257	- Resolve execution-intent source during `ddx try` / `ddx work`.
258	- Attach `execution-routing-intent` evidence before execution.
259	- Update result/evidence tests.
260	
261	Acceptance:
262	
263	1. Attempts with `tier:smart` record source `bead_hint`.
264	2. CLI routing flags record source `cli`.
265	3. Heuristic inference records source `heuristic`.
266	4. Actual route facts are linked to requested intent when the attempt finishes.
267	
268	### Bead 4: expose audit metrics
269	
270	Scope:
271	
272	- Extend the TD-032 normalized attempt projection with routing-intent fields.
273	- Add rollup dimensions/counters for requested tier, source, degradation, and
274	  rejected pins.
275	
276	Acceptance:
277	
278	1. Metrics can report smart-hinted attempt count over a time window.
279	2. Metrics can list smart-hinted beads and justifications.
280	3. Metrics can compare success/cost for bead-hinted vs heuristic/default work.
281	
282	### Bead 5: update bead authoring guidance
283	
284	Scope:
285	
286	- Update bead authoring docs and DDx skills.
287	- Add examples for justified smart hints and rejected cargo-cult pins.
288	
289	Acceptance:
290	
291	1. Bead authoring template explains when to use `tier:smart`.
292	2. DDx skill guidance says not to persist harness/provider/model choices.
293	3. Skill validation passes.
294	
295	## Non-Goals
296	
297	- No durable bead-level concrete harness/provider/model pins.
298	- No DDx-side concrete route ranking or fallback.
299	- No change to Fizeau's routing algorithm.
300	- No materialized metrics store in the first implementation.
301	- No automatic promotion to `tier:smart` solely because a previous attempt used
302	  a particular harness or model.
303	
304	## Open Questions
305	
306	- Should `tier:smart` without justification be a hard blocker immediately, or
307	  start as a warning for one release?
308	- Should `tier:*` remain labels long term, or should DDx eventually expose a
309	  first-class `execution-tier` field while continuing to read labels for
310	  compatibility?
311	- What exact power floor should each tier map to once Fizeau catalog power
312	  numbers are stable enough to document?
313

2	
3	import (
4		"testing"
5		"time"
6	
7		"github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx/internal/bead"
8		"github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx/internal/escalation"
9		"github.com/stretchr/testify/assert"
10		"github.com/stretchr/testify/require"
11	)
12	
13	// TestZeroConfigRetryEscalationPolicy verifies the retry/escalation policy
14	// composes correctly when the loop starts from the new metadata-driven
15	// "profile=cheap by default" path (commit 3ddf3d52, ddx-b2c9a245). It is a
16	// follow-up to AC6 of ddx-b790449b: confirm that transient failures retry
17	// within the cheap tier (no escalation) and substantive failures escalate
18	// cheap -> standard -> smart.
19	//
20	// The test drives a simulated tier ladder using the real escalation
21	// primitives (InferTier, IsInfrastructureFailure, ShouldEscalate,
22	// BuildEscalationSummary). It does not require a running
23	// agent service or harness binary.
24	func TestZeroConfigRetryEscalationPolicy(t *testing.T) {
25		// 1. Unflagged bead: no tier label, no kind, short description.
26		// InferTier must default to cheap so the loop starts at the cheap
27		// tier instead of the previous always-cheap fallback or the SD-024
28		// "no profile" path.
29		b := &bead.Bead{
30			ID:          "ddx-zero-config-001",
31			Title:       "trivial cleanup, unflagged",
32			Description: "minor doc tweak",
33		}
34		startTier := escalation.InferTier(b)
35		require.Equal(t, escalation.TierCheap, startTier,
36			"unflagged bead must infer cheap tier (zero-config default)")
37		require.Equal(t, "cheap", string(startTier),
38			"cheap tier maps to 'cheap' profile string consumed by LoadAndResolve")
39	
40		// 2. Transient infrastructure failure on cheap tier (e.g. 502 from
41		// the inference host). The policy is: do NOT escalate; defer with a
42		// retry-after and try the same tier again. The model wasn't given
43		// a fair chance, so escalation budget must not be consumed.
44		transientStatus := ExecuteBeadStatusExecutionFailed
45		transientDetail := "provider 502 Bad Gateway: connection refused"
46		require.True(t,
47			escalation.IsInfrastructureFailure(transientStatus, transientDetail),
48			"502 Bad Gateway on execution_failed must be classified as infrastructure failure")
49		require.True(t,
50			escalation.ShouldEscalate(transientStatus),
51			"execution_failed itself remains escalatable; the loop must consult IsInfrastructureFailure first to defer-vs-escalate")
52	
53		// Loop ordering invariant: when IsInfrastructureFailure is true the
54		// loop body defers (retry-after) instead of advancing the tier. The
55		// tier on the next attempt is therefore unchanged.
56		tierAfterTransient := startTier
57		require.Equal(t, escalation.TierCheap, tierAfterTransient,
58			"transient failure must not advance the tier ladder")
59	
60		// 3. Substantive failure on cheap tier (e.g. tests failed). Policy:
61		// escalate to the next tier (standard).
62		substantiveStatus := ExecuteBeadStatusExecutionFailed
63		substantiveDetail := "TestFoo failed: assertion mismatch"
64		require.False(t,
65			escalation.IsInfrastructureFailure(substantiveStatus, substantiveDetail),
66			"a model-capability failure detail must not match infrastructure patterns")
67		require.True(t,
68			escalation.ShouldEscalate(substantiveStatus),
69			"substantive execution_failed must escalate")
70		tierAfterCheapSubstantive := nextTier(tierAfterTransient)
71		require.Equal(t, escalation.TierStandard, tierAfterCheapSubstantive,
72			"cheap -> standard escalation step")
73	
74		// 4. Substantive failure on standard tier. Policy: escalate to smart.
75		standardStatus := ExecuteBeadStatusPostRunCheckFailed
76		require.True(t,
77			escalation.ShouldEscalate(standardStatus),
78			"post_run_check_failed escalates")
79		tierAfterStandardSubstantive := nextTier(tierAfterCheapSubstantive)
80		require.Equal(t, escalation.TierSmart, tierAfterStandardSubstantive,
81			"standard -> smart escalation step")
82	
83		// 5. Smart tier succeeds. Build the escalation summary and verify
84		// the full trail is recorded with winning_tier=smart and the cheap
85		// transient attempt counted as wasted-but-not-escalating-budget.
86		attempts := []escalation.TierAttemptRecord{
87			{Tier: string(escalation.TierCheap), Harness: "agent", Model: "cheap-model", Status: transientStatus, CostUSD: 0.0, DurationMS: 800},
88			{Tier: string(escalation.TierCheap), Harness: "agent", Model: "cheap-model", Status: substantiveStatus, CostUSD: 0.02, DurationMS: 1500},
89			{Tier: string(escalation.TierStandard), Harness: "codex", Model: "standard-model", Status: standardStatus, CostUSD: 0.15, DurationMS: 3400},
90			{Tier: string(escalation.TierSmart), Harness: "claude", Model: "smart-model", Status: escalation.SuccessStatus, CostUSD: 0.80, DurationMS: 9000},
91		}
92		summary := escalation.BuildEscalationSummary(attempts, string(escalation.TierSmart))
93		require.Equal(t, string(escalation.TierSmart), summary.WinningTier,
94			"smart tier won the escalation")
95		require.Len(t, summary.TiersAttempted, 4,
96			"trail records all attempts including the deferred-transient cheap attempt")
97		assert.InDelta(t, 0.97, summary.TotalCostUSD, 1e-9)
98		assert.InDelta(t, 0.17, summary.WastedCostUSD, 1e-9,
99			"cheap-substantive (0.02) + standard (0.15) wasted; cheap-transient billed $0; smart succeeded")
100		assert.Equal(t, string(escalation.TierCheap), summary.TiersAttempted[0].Tier,
101			"first attempt was cheap (the new metadata-driven default)")
102		assert.Equal(t, string(escalation.TierSmart), summary.TiersAttempted[3].Tier,
103			"final attempt was smart (top of ladder)")
104	}
105	
106	// TestZeroConfigEscalationSummaryEventBody verifies the escalation summary
107	// is emitted as a kind:escalation-summary bead event when the cheap-default
108	// ladder runs to completion. This is the operator-visible artifact of the
109	// retry/escalation policy.
110	func TestZeroConfigEscalationSummaryEventBody(t *testing.T) {
111		store := bead.NewStore(t.TempDir())
112		require.NoError(t, store.Init())
113		target := &bead.Bead{ID: "ddx-zero-config-002", Title: "cheap-default ladder", Priority: 0}
114		require.NoError(t, store.Create(target))
115	
116		attempts := []escalation.TierAttemptRecord{
117			{Tier: string(escalation.TierCheap), Status: ExecuteBeadStatusExecutionFailed, CostUSD: 0.01, DurationMS: 1000},
118			{Tier: string(escalation.TierStandard), Status: ExecuteBeadStatusExecutionFailed, CostUSD: 0.10, DurationMS: 2000},
119			{Tier: string(escalation.TierSmart), Status: escalation.SuccessStatus, CostUSD: 0.50, DurationMS: 4000},
120		}
121		require.NoError(t,
122			escalation.AppendEscalationSummaryEvent(
123				store, target.ID, "test-worker", attempts, string(escalation.TierSmart), time.Unix(1, 0).UTC()))
124	
125		events, err := store.Events(target.ID)
126		require.NoError(t, err)
127		var found *bead.BeadEvent
128		for i := range events {
129			if events[i].Kind == "escalation-summary" {
130				found = &events[i]
131				break
132			}
133		}
134		require.NotNil(t, found, "escalation-summary event must be appended for the cheap-default ladder run")
135		assert.Contains(t, found.Summary, "winning_tier=smart")
136		assert.Contains(t, found.Summary, "attempts=3")
137		assert.Contains(t, found.Body, `"tier":"cheap"`,
138			"summary body records cheap as the first-attempted tier (zero-config default)")
139		assert.Contains(t, found.Body, `"tier":"standard"`)
140		assert.Contains(t, found.Body, `"tier":"smart"`)
141	}
142	
143	// nextTier advances one rung up the cheap -> standard -> smart ladder.
144	// Mirrors the implicit ladder used by the execute-loop when escalating
145	// beyond a substantive failure. Smart is the top; further calls stay
146	// at smart (callers exhaust escalation when ShouldEscalate fires at smart).
147	func nextTier(t escalation.ModelTier) escalation.ModelTier {
148		switch t {
149		case escalation.TierCheap:
150			return escalation.TierStandard
151		case escalation.TierStandard:
152			return escalation.TierSmart
153		default:
154			return escalation.TierSmart
155		}
156	}

1	# Bead Authoring Template
2	
3	> A bead's description + AC must be sufficient context for a competent
4	> sub-agent to execute it without hand-curation.
5	> — Reliability principle **P7** (`docs/helix/06-iterate/reliability-principles.md`)
6	
7	This document is the canonical template + rubric for creating beads in
8	this repository. It exists because the 2026-05-03 bead-quality audit
9	(`.ddx/executions/20260503T155638-bead57f0cb9e/bead-quality-audit-2026-05-03.md`)
10	found that only **1 of 20** representative open beads scored 8/8 on
11	the sufficient-sub-agent-prompt rubric. The most common failure modes
12	were: no specific test names in AC (80 % miss), no `go test` +
13	lefthook gate in AC (80 % miss), and no root cause with file:line in
14	description (65 % miss).
15	
16	---
17	
18	## 1. The 8-criterion rubric
19	
20	Every bead must satisfy:
21	
22	| # | Criterion | Verifier |
23	|---|-----------|----------|
24	| (a) | **Title**: one-line scope clarity, imperative, names subsystem + change | "Pagination off-by-one in bead list endpoint" not "Pagination bug" |
25	| (b) | **Description**: problem + ROOT CAUSE WITH FILE:LINE + proposed fix + non-scope | Root cause cites `cli/path/file.go:42-50` |
26	| (c) | **AC**: numbered, verifiable, includes specific test names | At least one AC names `TestFoo` or a `go test -run` filter |
27	| (d) | **AC** includes "wired-in" assertion when introducing new code paths | New function → AC asserts a caller invokes it (production or test) |
28	| (e) | **AC** includes `go test` command + lefthook gate | Both `cd cli && go test ./...` and `lefthook run pre-commit` named |
29	| (f) | **Labels**: phase + area + kind + cross-refs | `phase:N`, `area:*`, `kind:*`, plus adr/spec/prevention if applicable |
30	| (g) | **Parent + Deps**: explicit | Parent set; deps either listed or "no deps" stated |
31	| (h) | Description reads as **sufficient sub-agent prompt** | A competent agent given only the bead body can pick a file and run tests without asking |
32	
33	Score 8/8 = execution-ready. Less = retrofit before dispatch.
34	
35	---
36	
37	## 2. Template fields
38	
39	```text
40	TITLE
41	  <area>: <imperative one-line scope>
42	  e.g. "agent: unify ExecuteLoopSpec across cobra/HTTP/server layers"
43	
44	TYPE
45	  task | bug | epic | chore
46	
47	LABELS
48	  phase:<N>, area:<subsystem>, kind:<category>
49	  + cross-refs: prevention, adr:<id>, spec:<id>, story:<n>, refactor
50	
51	PARENT
52	  <ddx-id> of governing epic / story / spec
53	  (omit only if this is a root)
54	
55	DEPS
56	  - <ddx-id-1>: <one-line why this dep matters>
57	  - <ddx-id-2>: <one-line why this dep matters>
58	  (or explicit "No deps.")
59	
60	SPEC-ID (when applicable)
61	  --set spec-id=FEAT-NNN  | SD-NNN | TD-NNN | ADR-NNN
62	
63	DESCRIPTION
64	  PROBLEM
65	    <one-paragraph statement of what is broken or missing>
66	
67	  ROOT CAUSE
68	    <function/file:line where the defect lives>
69	    <what the code does today vs what it should do>
70	    For new features: ROOT CAUSE -> "no existing implementation"
71	    is OK; replace with CURRENT STATE describing the gap.
72	
73	  PROPOSED FIX
74	    <bulleted concrete changes; cite file:line for each>
75	    - cli/internal/foo/bar.go:120-145  rename Baz -> Quux
76	    - cli/internal/foo/bar_test.go      add TestQuux_RoundTrip
77	
78	  NON-SCOPE
79	    - <thing readers might assume is included but isn't>
80	    - <thing covered by sister bead ddx-NNNN>

80	    - <thing covered by sister bead ddx-NNNN>
81	
82	  INTERSECTIONS (optional)
83	    - <ddx-id>: <how this bead relates to that one>
84	
85	ACCEPTANCE
86	  1. <verifiable assertion 1 — names a Test, file path, command, or
87	     observable artifact>
88	  2. <verifiable assertion 2>
89	  3. ...
90	  N-1. cd cli && go test ./<paths>/... green.
91	  N. lefthook run pre-commit passes.
92	```
93	
94	---
95	
96	## 3. Examples
97	
98	### 3.1 Example of a strong bead (audit score 8/8)
99	
100	`ddx-1e516bc9` — *"fizeau Execute: explicit Harness + empty Model + non-empty Profile/MinPower must route within harness's eligible models"*
101	
102	Why it scored 8/8:
103	
104	- **Title (a)** names subsystem (`fizeau Execute`), the precondition (`explicit Harness + empty Model`), and the expected behavior (`route within harness's eligible models`).
105	- **Description (b)** opens with **OBSERVED** (one paragraph, file:line) → **SCOPE — UPSTREAM (fizeau) ONLY** → **INVESTIGATION FIRST** (with explicit close-as-no-op outcome) → **UPSTREAM CHANGE** (file:line into `service_execute.go:193-224`, `models.yaml:31-60`, `registry.go`) → explicit **NOT IN SCOPE** list naming five excluded paths.
106	- **AC (c)** names two concrete tests:
107	  `TestExecute_ExplicitHarnessEmptyModelWithProfile_RoutesWithinHarness` and
108	  `TestExecute_ExplicitHarnessEmptyModelNoProfile_FailsClearly`.
109	- **Wired-in (d)**: AC #6 asserts the resolved model surfaces in
110	  `.ddx/workers/<id>/spec.json`, `.ddx/executions/<run-id>/result.json`, and the
111	  workers UI — three distinct call-graph endpoints.
112	- **Test command + lefthook (e)**: AC #9 — `cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/... ./cmd/... green; lefthook pre-commit passes.`
113	- **Labels (f)**: `phase:2, area:agent, kind:fix, upstream-fizeau, observed-failure`.
114	- **Parent + Deps (g)**: parent `ddx-e34994e2`; dep `ddx-cfedee8e` with explanation that the ladder must supply MinPower for fizeau to act on.
115	- **Sufficient (h)**: a sub-agent given only the bead body knows which file in fizeau to read, what the gap looks like, what the negative-test outcome should be, and how to verify the end-to-end fix without asking.
116	
117	### 3.2 Example of a weak bead (audit score 1/8) — annotated
118	
119	`ddx-0793ac75` — *"federation: federated GraphQL resolvers + routing-metadata fields"*
120	
121	Verbatim description:
122	```
123	GraphQL resolvers that aggregate across spokes (federatedWorkers,
124	federatedRuns, federatedDocuments, etc.). Add routing-metadata fields
125	exposing which spoke owns each result. Write-routing contract: hub
126	forwards mutations to owning node, never broadcasts (Story 15
127	dependency).
128	```
129	
130	Verbatim AC:
131	```
132	1. federated* resolvers in schema.
133	2. Routing-metadata visible per result.
134	3. Mutations route to owner; tested.
135	4. Tests cover happy + degraded paths.
136	```
137	
138	Failures, criterion by criterion:
139	
140	- **(b) FAIL** — no problem statement (what's broken today?), no root cause, no file:line, no non-scope. The reader cannot tell where the schema lives, where resolvers go, or what "routing-metadata" should look like as a Go type.
141	- **(c) FAIL** — "tested" and "Tests cover happy + degraded paths" name no `Test*` function. Sub-agent has no way to know what to write or where.
142	- **(d) PARTIAL** — "tested" implies the resolvers are wired, but no production-call-graph assertion (e.g., "schema.graphql Mutation block exposes field X").
143	- **(e) FAIL** — no `go test` command, no lefthook gate.
144	- **(h) FAIL** — a sub-agent reading this cold cannot answer: which schema file? which resolver package? what fields on routing-metadata? what's the wire format? Hand-curation by the operator is the only path forward.
145	
146	**Retrofit shape** (illustrative — not an actual edit):
147	```
148	PROBLEM
149	  Story 14 needs aggregate-across-spokes resolvers. Today the GraphQL
150	  schema (cli/internal/server/graphql/schema.graphql) exposes only
151	  per-node Worker/Run/Document queries. Federation requires
152	  federated* counterparts that fan out to spokes and stitch results.
153	
154	ROOT CAUSE
155	  No federated resolver layer exists. cli/internal/server/graphql_resolver.go
156	  contains only single-node resolvers. Spoke fan-out is in
157	  cli/internal/server/federation_spoke.go but nothing in the GraphQL
158	  layer calls it.
159	
160	PROPOSED FIX
161	  - schema.graphql: add Federated{Worker,Run,Document} types with
162	    `ownerSpoke: String!` field.
163	  - graphql_resolver.go: add federatedWorkers/federatedRuns/
164	    federatedDocuments resolvers calling federation_spoke.FanOut.
165	  - graphql_mutation.go: write-routing helper routes mutations to
166	    owning node via federation_spoke.RouteToOwner.
167	
168	NON-SCOPE
169	  - New non-federated resolvers (out: covered by Story 13).
170	  - Spoke discovery (out: covered by ddx-fe63969f).
171	
172	ACCEPTANCE
173	  1. schema.graphql exposes federatedWorkers/federatedRuns/
174	     federatedDocuments queries with ownerSpoke metadata.
175	  2. TestFederatedWorkers_FanOutAggregates verifies fan-out + stitch.
176	  3. TestFederatedMutation_RoutesToOwner verifies write-routing.
177	  4. TestFederatedWorkers_DegradedSpoke returns partial results +
178	     warning.
179	  5. cd cli && go test ./internal/server/... green.

211	- **`/tmp/` references as load-bearing context.** 5 of 20 audited beads cite a `/tmp/...` plan file. These are functionally broken links to a sub-agent on a different machine. **Inline the excerpt.**

input.new_string:
## 7. Tier-hint label: `tier:hint=<name>`

Add a `tier:hint=<name>` label to tell the execute-loop which power tier to
start from. This is an operator-level hint for beads where the default
cheap-first start is likely to waste a full cheap attempt.

| Label | Resolved floor | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| `tier:hint=cheap` | 0 (unconstrained, same as default) | Mechanical tasks; cheap models expected to succeed |
| `tier:hint=standard` | Second viable floor in catalog | Ordinary implementation work; cheap tier likely to fail |
| `tier:hint=smart` | Highest viable floor in catalog | Hard/broad/architecture-sensitive work; requires strong reasoning |

**Composition with `--min-power`:** the label sets a floor. The `--min-power`
flag can raise the floor further but cannot lower it below the label value.

**Invalid tier names** (anything other than `cheap`, `standard`, `smart`) are
silently ignored and the default floor is used.

`tier:hint=<name>` is distinct from `tier:smart` / `tier:standard` / `tier:cheap`
(the older heuristic-override labels). The `tier:hint=` prefix marks it as an
explicit floor hint; the plain `tier:` prefix is still recognized by `InferTier`
for heuristic routing. Both can appear on the same bead.

---

## 8. References

- `docs/helix/06-iterate/reliability-principles.md` — P7 source.
- `.ddx/executions/20260503T155638-bead57f0cb9e/bead-quality-audit-2026-05-03.md` — the audit that produced this template.
- `.agents/skills/ddx/reference/beads.md` and `.claude/skills/ddx/reference/beads.md` — operational guidance on creating beads via `ddx bead create`.
